sentences.text: In 19-- I am going to go when I was 14 years old, in 1938.
I was in the schoolyard.
It was, I think so.
And we started school September, like.
And then, suddenly, a siren went up.
And we had to leave the school.
On the radios, we heard that Hitler-- march-- Hitler's troops march over the borders, and they are taking the Sudetenland.
That was the Western part of Czechoslovakia.
Then, our school bus closed.
And when I came home, I heard already talking about, you know, what happened.
And the mean time, what I recall, even before '38, we had quite lots of people staying in our house overnight.
Jews from Poland were already crossing the border and escaping from Poland, because they probably were more aware, or people who-- who knew what went on since 1933 in Germany and were escaping.
And they came through Slovakia and went further down to Hungary and to Italy, because what I heard, they are going to Palestine.
So--
At home, because those people were in our house-- sometimes family with children, sometimes only single people.
Those were refugees who already left their homeland, Poland.
But you know, when you're 14, I didn't pay too much attention, because my only [LAUGHS] concern was, from school, get home, throw the books away, and go and meet my friends.
So you know, what a teenager-- and I was not interested in these politics.
But then, you know, when we left school, soon after, you know, they kicked out all the Jewish children from school.
So that was the last time. 1938 I couldn't attend a public school-- no more.
And Slovakia became, you know, when Hitler took the Sudeten-- Slovakia became an independent state, Nazi-oriented, with the head of the state a Catholic priest, Dr.
Josef Tico.
T-I-C-O-- Tico, not Tito.

And you know, the propaganda, the loudspeakers, and you know, defacing the Jewish stores with Stars of David-- in the center of the main street where our house was, was big letters, you know, with white paint-- Jews and Gypsies, the same-- you deserve-- you know, I'm just translating from Slovak, which was in rhyme-- you deserve a concentration camp.
I never heard about concentration camp.
I don't-- didn't know what's the meaning of it.
But suddenly, our lives turned upside down.
Since we lived on the main street, and our house was there, Jews were not allowed to live on the main street.
So we were relocated to the back of the city-- still not a ghetto-- where, with other families, we shared a little place, a house.
And then it was curfew.
We could go out only a few hours a day.
Since my father-- you know, he-- his business went bankrupt already in '36.
So at this time, he was already a Hebrew teacher-- like you say in Hebrew, [HEBREW].
So he didn't earn too much money, didn't have too much savings.
So my brothers-- and because I have to come back still with my brothers-- they went and did farm work.
And for return, they brought home some food.
So we lived with this, you know, quite a while-- and the slogans, and the propaganda, and the loud speakers.
But the worst thing was my good friends with whom I went to school and with whom we share-- I shared everything.
I am talking personally, my experience-- they were not my friends anymore.
They didn't want to do nothing with me.
They singled me out, pointed at me.
You dirty Jew.
And the whole city-- I mean, the Christian city-- was against us.
You know, probably, how Europe was.
Europe, either-- and mainly in the part in Slovakia where we were living-- either you are a Christian or a Jew-- no other religions-- absolutely no.
Christian mean there were many-- not many-- few other denomination.
But Jews, you know, whatever-- we were non grata personas.
So we had to live with that.
But it was very, very painful and very shocking for me.
Suddenly, you know, like to say overnight, such a change.
What we start-- we tried to live how we could.
But like I say before, my-- we were alone-- my-- my three brother and me at home.
But in '38, my brother was already in Bratislava.
He was apprentice.
I mean, he was already not apprentice, he was working as a-- upholsterer.
And in Bratislava was the-- how you say-- the-- [NON-ENGLISH] the [NON-ENGLISH].
The Jews who were not born there have to leave to their place of birth.
So my brother came back home, the other brother too.
He was in the yeshiva in-- near [NON-ENGLISH] quite a-- about 200 miles away.
And we all were back with our father and my stepmother at this time in 1939, 1940.
And like I say, food was scarce.
Every day, you know, we didn't know what's going to happen.
Rumors were that, probably, we won't be able stay there.
They will take us and resettle us.
They talked about, you know, in Africa, it is, Madagascar [NON-ENGLISH] you know?
And we had-- I had two aunts in Palestine.
In 1933, when Hitler came to power, my two aunts went to Palestine.
And one aunt who lived in Essen came back to Czechoslovakia.
Because this time, all Jews who were not natives or didn't-- in Germany, they kicked them out too, through the borders, if somebody was Polish descent, or Czech descent.
So she came back.
And my two uncles, my father's two brothers, left.
I don't know where.
I don't think she was born-- also with the yeshiva to Palestine.
So my father thought maybe it's still time we can go there.
But since no money, and Jews' travel-- Jewish travel was restricted, so he couldn't make it.
So we lived this way until 1942, off and on food, and-- and tragedies.
And people-- some people committed suicide.
And I have to say also, quite a few in our community converted to Catholicism.
Then people who had money, not too many, maybe us, I don't know, were in hiding, went to the forest.
Or some-- by some Catholic family, if they had money, they were hiding them.
But it was not the case in our family. 1942, one night, suddenly, knock, knock, on the door.
And the Hlinka Guard-- Hlinka Guard in Slovakia was formed when, you know, Slovakia was-- became independent state.
And the Guard was equivalent to the SS in Germany.
So they knocked, and they picked up the girls, 15 to 26, from their own home.
It was at night.
Then they-- and I was one of them, because I was in this age around-- age range.
And then, they picked us up and loaded us on trucks.
And we left the city.
We could take along only a few little things.
And we traveled overnight to a city, Poprd, which was on the Slovak-Polish border.
P-O-P-R-D, Poprd.
We were kind of bewildered.
But the guard, the Hlinka Guard, promised-- told us-- promised us-- that we are taken to work, to Germany so we can help our families who are behind and support them.
So it was not [NON-ENGLISH].
Since we were young-- so we said, oh, that'd be wonderful, because you know, it was horrible already-- no money, no food.
So we went.
But, you know, leaving your home-- home base-- home city-- is very hard.
So that was the last time I saw my parents.
It was March 24, 1942.
And we traveled overnight to this camp where they concentrated the girls.
They needed 1,000.
And from the whole-- from the cities which surrounded us, from Presov, Kosice, Bardejov and so, they brought in with trucks to this-- to this city, the girls.
And March 26, we had to line up, 5 in rows, and march very early in the morning-- so the rest of the population wouldn't see us-- to the railroad station.
And far away, we saw already a long track of cattle cars.
We didn't even think of it that those are suited for us-- [LAUGHS] --for we are human beings.
But what a disappointment.
We came there, and the guards started screaming.
The cattle cars opened.
In Slovakian, actually, up, up, you-- you dirty Jewish whores, and so on.
So they pushed about 60 to 80 of us in one cattle car.
In the center of the cattle car were two buckets of water.
One-- no-- one was full of water, and the other was empty.
They locked the cattle car, and there we went-- unknown where.
We thought we are traveling to Germany for work, but what a disappointment.
On the borders-- Polish border, the train stopped, because the-- the guards gave the-- the-- the whole transport over to the SS.
And you know, since I was so tiny and thin-- I was this time, maybe, 110 pounds-- the tall, big girls lifted me up to look what's going on there.
So I saw only the exchange of guards.
And I said-- and there, I saw a big sign in Polish.
I don't know what the city is-- or what station it was.
And I said, girls, we are in Poland.
Maybe through Poland we go to Germany.
But not-- they didn't open the-- the-- you know, the cattle cars so to empty, which you know, the-- with the bucket which already spilled over.
Water was gone.
But we had-- in no time, we went again.
In maybe four or five hours-- I don't know the time-- the-- the train stopped in the middle of a field-- nothing.
And they opened all, you know, the cattle car.
And the SS started screaming, [NON-ENGLISH] in the middle of the field.
And it was so high, and a ditch underneath.
So we had to jump, because it was very high.
And the SS screaming and yelling.
And in five [NON-ENGLISH] five rows.
And we-- we-- you know-- we were bewildered.
And then we had to march.
We saw-- I saw-- we all saw a huge flat land in front of us where we march, with little, flickering lights, surrounded with barbed wire, and some little, like-- like boxes.
When we came closer, we saw the barbed wire, and we saw the barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And then, we came and marched through an iron gate.
The gate opened.
On top, [NON-ENGLISH] On the left side was a huge brick building with a huge chimney.
So we-- we whispered to each other, that's the factory where we are going to work.
And then we walked into the camp.
I have to mention, before we came, no women ever were in Auschwitz.
We were the first 1,000 women who ever entered the gates of hell there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only men-- and they didn't build even a camp for women.
From the men's camp, in the main camp, Auschwitz, they took 10 barracks.
And the-- you know-- the 10 barracks were-- separated with barbed wire for us.
But when we came into the camp, they were already standing in line, women with striped uniforms.
One day before we came, 1,000 women from Ravensbruck, but Germans, they brought them there.
They were Germans who were sentenced, mostly, for life in prison for their crimes committed.
So they'd spent-- and they-- they-- I mean spent their time in Ravensbruck.
Ravensbruck was a women's camp for criminals, but it was there already a long time.
And those women were assigned to be our kapos, our foremen, our vorarbeiters.
But we stared at them.
But they looked pretty normal, only striped human.
Their hair-- they had hair-- nothing.
And then, we-- they lined us up in front of those 10 barracks.
And the last barrack was barrack 10.
And we were assigned, the 1,000 women, to the last barrack.
So they counted us and over counted us, and we were staying hours and hours outside.
SS men came.
Functionaries came.
And then, those Germans, the prisoner women, started walking around now and looking us over.
But we still had our civilian clothes and our little packages.
But they ordered all the packages, all the luggage what we brought-- luggage-- brought around to put it on the pile between barrack 10 and 9.
We had to throw it in.
But still, we had our uniform-- our civilian clothing.
So after a long time, they let us into the barrack.
But to get into a barrack-- and we didn't have to stay, you know, in the freezing weather.
You know, in March, in this part of Poland, is bitter cold-- snowflakes and frost-- awful.
And we started to get in, you know, into the barrack.
That-- you know, the door was not too wide.
And we started pushing.
Everybody wanted to get warm.
So I pushed too, naturally.
I have good elbows.
And with the group where we pushed in, the-- the German kapos were already standing.
And they have whips.
[NON-ENGLISH] And there were small rooms in the barrack, dormitories, maybe 10 by 12, or maybe larger.
So about-- I-- I think, 80 women or so were pushed in.
And it was no bed, no stool, no nothing, no heat.
So we had to sit close-- one close to another.
But we were so thirsty.
And we needed to go to the bathroom.
And can you imagine 1,000 women, bewildered, after a long journey, in a strange place, and water, and toilet?
But the water was only in the basement.
It was, you know, these buildings, these barracks were built, I think so, in 1917 as-- as-- you know, [NON-ENGLISH].
How you say?
For soldiers' barracks.
Barracks.
And in the basement was you know, a long pipe.
And the water was dripping.
And toilets were about 10.
So everybody wanted to get to the water.
Everybody wanted to go to the bathroom. 10 o'clock was [NON-ENGLISH] And you had to go back to your dormitory, to your little place.
And it was Babylon.
Everybody pushed.
Everybody screamed.
So finally, I get to the water.
I hardly made it at 10 o'clock back to my dormitory.
But still, it was cold.
We were crying, shivering.
And finally, somehow, we went to sleep. 4 o'clock in the morning, screaming, [NON-ENGLISH], knocking on every bed.
So we had to rush out for say appell, for roll call.
And we did that too.
Then again, the SS and the kapos and all-- we were standing from 4:00 in the morning, until it was good light.
And then, the SS-- I don't know if it was Drexler, because she was the lagerfuhrer in the SS guard, the SS lagerfuhrer-- and Fraulein Drexler.
And then, after, you know, maybe it was already close to dawn, we didn't get to eat nothing.
Only later, they brought kettle with water, little, lukewarm, dark water.
So-- so we got a little tea.
I don't recall if I got it or not.
But then they separated 50 from the 1,000-- 999.
In the middle again, between the two barracks, they built a tent.
And the 50 of us had to line up, one by one, still in our civilian clothes, and marching into the tent.
On the side-- the right side of the tent were SS women in uniforms.
So one by one, [NON-ENGLISH]-- undress.
Everything undress, but you know, in the bitter cold.
So we undressed.
Then the last women-- a little while-- examined us, you know, if we are not hiding anything-- you know, nude.
A gynecology-- or I don't know-- examined us.
And then they shaved us, our hair everywhere.
And in the middle of-- of the tent was a pool, kind of, with the water, dark water.
Had to jump into the water, and dip in the water, the cold water.
It was a cleansing bath, because we Jews, they said, brought lice to the camp, and we are dirty.
When you just, you know, for a few seconds, dip completely after the shaving.
And then they threw at me a-- a Russian uniform.
I have to mention-- before we came, 75,000 Russian POWs were, since 1940, in Birkenau.
And they were killed.
And we got their uniforms.
The uniforms still were stiff from blood.

And you know, those uniforms, those were long blouses-- I got a-- you know-- a long blouse-- and you know how thin I am-- and the pants, you know, like for-- they were here wider-- probably like cavalry with horses wore those-- and I didn't-- no underwear-- nothing.
And wooden clogs.
And one red bowl and a spoon.
That's it.
We have to wear it.
It was thrown at us.
The blouse was so big for me, it was all the way down to the floor.
The pants, I pulled up-- were here.
I didn't have a choice-- and nothing to-- to hold it.
And nothing to cover our head-- no stocking.
And we had to line up until all the 1,000 were cleansed.
And then we could go back to the barrack.
It took, you know, I don't know how long.
Finally, it happened.
The girls-- we-- I have-- I have my tattoo number-- when I was tattoo-- is 1,173.
I explain you.
The 1,000 what you have, they were-- we were the first women tattooed.
The 1,000 are the Germans who came from Ravensbruck.
I am the 173rd Jewish woman who ever entered Auschwitz.
Jewish woman.
Jewish-- because later, non-Jewish came too.
The criminals.
We were from Slovakia, the first 1,000 Jewish women who were brought to Auschwitz.
--73rd Jewish woman.
Yes, Jewish woman.
The Germans and the Aryan were not tattooed.
They got numbers, but not tattooed.
Only the Jews were tattooed.
So you know, I don't know if it's-- if it's mentioned somewhere, this.
But except in the I'm sure it is.
But really, people know that non-Jewish women got not-- not tattooed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
